coredata.dc:title: Municipal sludge-derived carbon dots-decorated, N-doped hierarchical biocarbon for the electrochemical reduction of carbon dioxide
coredata.dc:creator: Deng, Lifang
Yuan, Haoran
Qian, Xin
Lu, Qiang
Wang, Lufeng
Hu, Huawen
Chen, Yong
coredata.dc:description: Uncontrolled emission of carbon dioxide (CO2) from various industries, which poses a severe threat to the environment and induces climate change, CO2 electroreduction is an advanced technology for converting waste CO2 and closing anthropogenic carbon cycle. However, developing novel, affordable and high-performance electrocatalysts for converting CO2 to value-added hydrocarbons remains highly challenging. Herein, a carbon dots-decorated, N-doped hierarchical biocarbon material is developed from municipal sludge, which can work as an effective and robust carbocatalyst for the electrochemical reduction of CO2. More importantly, such hierarchical biocarbon catalyzes the electrochemical conversion of CO2 to value-added organic products, including methane, methanol and ethanol, with excellent stability and total Faraday efficiency of 90.14% at about -0.71 V vs. RHE. Reasons for the carbocatalyst electroactivity toward efficient and selective production of ethanol is proposed to be the carbon dots and the doped N sites (especially pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N), which is also validated by density functional theory calculations (DFT). A possible route for the electrochemical reduction of CO2 is also unraveled. This study is a significant step forward to the exploitation of biomass wastes for harvesting greenhouse gasses and electrochemical energy conversion.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a-d) SEM images of (a) MSC, (b) MSC-A, (c) MSC-H, and (d) MSC-HA; insets of (b) and (d) display high-resolution SEM images of MSC-A and MSC-HA, respectively. e) TEM image of MSC-HA (inset shows the HRTEM image of a CD). f) SAED pattern of MSC-HA (inset presents the photo image of the remaining solution collected after the hydrothermal treatment under white ray (left) and UV stream of 365 nm (right)). g) Raman spectra of MSC, MSC-H, MSC-A and MSC-HA. h) High-resolution XPS spectra of N 1 s in MSC, MSC-H, MSC-A, and MSC-HA. i) Four configurations of N in the municipal sludge-based biocarbon materials, including MSC, MSC-H, MSC-A, and MSC-HA, as calculated based on the deconvoluted peaks in the high-resolution XPS N1s spectra.
Linear sweep voltammograms of MSC, MSC-A, MSC-H and MSC-HA in the Ar-saturated or CO2-saturated 0.1 mol/L KHCO3 electrolyte.

Production rates (a) and the corresponding Faraday efficiency (b) of CH3CH2OH, CH3OH, and CH4 with different catalysts, including MSC, MSC-A, MSC-H, and MSC-HA (at the external potential of – 0.71 V vs. RHE); Production rates (c) and faradaic efficiencies (d) of CH3CH2OH, CH3OH, and CH4 on the MSC-HA catalyst at the corresponding potential.

(a) Production yields and FEs at −0.71 V during recycling test repeated 6 times. (b) The curve of the production yield vs. reaction time at –0.71 V. (c) Production rates and FEs of MSC-HA at the potential of –0.71 V with alternating 5 h cycles between CO2 and Ar-saturated aqueous electrolytes over a total duration of 30 h. (d) Time-dependent current density curve for MSC-HA at –0.71 V vs. RHE.

DFT modeling of CO2 electrochemical reduction on MSC-HA. (a) Free energy diagram of the CO2 electrochemical conversion to C2H5OH on MSC-HA. (b) Energetically possible structures for the elementary procedures of CO2 electrochemical reduction on the (002) facet of MSC-HA (gray = C, red = O, white = H and blue = N).

(a) In-situ infrared spectrum hired for the CO2 electrocatalytic reduction on the MSC-HA-coated electrode at -0.71 V (vs. RHE) with electrolysis time (in the CO2-saturated KHCO3 electrolyte). (b) Schematic illustration of the catalysis of the electrochemical conversion of CO2 on carbocatalyst derived from the municipal sludge and the pathway toward the production of methane, methanol, and ethanol.
Specific surface area, the atomic content of pyridinic nitrogen as obtained by XPS, and the measurement of the activity for the electrochemical reduction of CO2 on the as-prepared catalysts.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Uncontrolled emission of carbon dioxide (CO2) from various industries, which poses a severe threat to the environment and induces climate change, CO2 electroreduction is an advanced technology for converting waste CO2 and closing anthropogenic carbon cycle. However, developing novel, affordable and high-performance electrocatalysts for converting CO2 to value-added hydrocarbons remains highly challenging. Herein, a carbon dots-decorated, N-doped hierarchical biocarbon material is developed from municipal sludge, which can work as an effective and robust carbocatalyst for the electrochemical reduction of CO2. More importantly, such hierarchical biocarbon catalyzes the electrochemical conversion of CO2 to value-added organic products, including methane, methanol and ethanol, with excellent stability and total Faraday efficiency of 90.14% at about -0.71 V vs. RHE. Reasons for the carbocatalyst electroactivity toward efficient and selective production of ethanol is proposed to be the carbon dots and the doped N sites (especially pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N), which is also validated by density functional theory calculations (DFT). A possible route for the electrochemical reduction of CO2 is also unraveled. This study is a significant step forward to the exploitation of biomass wastes for harvesting greenhouse gasses and electrochemical energy conversion.

ce:section.ce:para: Over past decades, the world-wide reliance on fossil fuels has produced a great quantity of CO2, a greenhouse gas (Liu et al., 2017). CO2 is a carbon product with the highest oxidation stage, which is close to "inert gas" by considering its thermodynamic and kinetic stability. Electrochemical reduction is a promising renewable method in converting CO2 into value-added commodity organic products (such as CH3COO−, CO, CH4, and CH3OH), which is one of the appealing CO2 utilization strategies for the management of the global carbon balance and potentially leads to a carbon-neutral world with a high pace of development (J. Wu et al., 2016). However, exploring chemically active and structurally stable electrocatalysts remains challenging, thus limiting the fast progress in CO2 electroreduction. Although electrocatalysts are primarily based on the precious metals (Pd, Ag, Pt and Au) (Gao et al., 2015; Guo et al., 2018; Ma et al., 2018; Shironita et al., 2018; Zhu 2013), the scarcity and high cost greatly restrain their applications. To address this, researchers have made much effort on investigating non-precious metals (e.g., Cu, Fe, Pb, Ni and Sn) and their complexes (Ma et al., 2016a; Saravanakumar et al., 2012; Satthawong et al., 2013; Zhao et al., 2019; Varley 2013; Chen et al., 2012; Rasul ei al., 2018; Hu et al., 2021). Although non-precious metal-based electrocatalysts (e.g., Cu-based ones) are effective in the electrochemical reduction of CO2 toward the production of hydrocarbons and oxygenates, their instability is an insurmountable obstacle to their practical applications.

Alternatively, metal-free electrocatalysts have recently attracted much attention due to their earth abundance, low cost, environmental friendliness, and tunability in the structure and surface properties. Consequently, many metal-free electrocatalysts have been reported, such as microorganism (Bajracharya et al., 2015), conjugated polymers (Aydin et al., 2004), covalent organic frameworks (Diercks et al., 2015; Diercks et al., 2018), aromatic anion radicals (Froehlich et al., 2015) and heteroatom-doped (B, P, N, S and F) carbon materials (Song et al., 2017; Kumar et al., 2013; Wu et al., 2015; Xie et al., 2018). Among these metal-free materials, heteroatom-doped carbon catalysts have been received the most significant attention (Wu et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2019), especially N-doped carbon, such as N-doped graphene quantum dots (J. Wu et al., 2016), N-doped mesoporous carbon (Song et al., 2017), N-doped carbon nanofiber (Kumar et al., 2013), N-doped carbon nanotubes (Wu et al., 2015; Ma et al., 2019; Tuci et al., 2018), N-doped nanocarbon (Jhong et al., 2016), and N-doped graphene-like carbon (Sun et al., 2016). The metal-free N-doped carbon has been revealed as an attractive candidate to metal-based ones for catalyzing the electrolysis of CO2. This is because N doping can effectively modify the electronic structure of the carbon framework due to the high electronegativity of N, e.g., breaking the homogeneous electronic structure and thus bringing active sites with polarization (Norouzi et al., 2020). Besides the benefits brought by N doping, the porous structure of the N-doped carbon is also conducive to achieving highly efficient CO2 electrocatalysis due to the promoted transport of reactants and electrolytes. Meanwhile, the electrical conductivity, wettability, charge transfer, charge-discharge rate, and durability might also be concurrently improved (Paraknowitsch et al., 2013; Inagaki et al., 2018; Fernandes et al., 2019). However, the conventional methods for the preparation of metal-free N-doped carbon are normally multistep, complex, and tedious. Moreover, they rely on hazardous and toxic chemicals as carbon and nitrogen sources. Sometimes, specific templates and special equipment are required. Thus, it is highly desirable to fabricate N-doped carbon materials in a facile, cost-effective, and environmentally benign manner for the economical and sustainable elctroreduction of CO2.

Naturally occurring biomass is a renewable, environmentally friendly, and abundant carbonaceous raw material, which can be used as feedstock to generate various value-added products and functional carbon-based materials. Notably, it natively contains abundant heteroatoms (such as P, N, and S), so it can work as carbon and heteroatom sources to prepare metal-free carbocatalysts by a simple conversion process (Deng et al., 2017). The self-sustained heteroatoms can avoid an additional procedure for heteroatom doping, thus making the preparation more straightforward and viable. Many potential applications have been explored based on biomass-derived carbon materials, such as microbial energy harvesting, supercapacitors, lithium-ion batteries, catalysts for oxygen reactions, and CO2 capture (Zhou et al., 2019). Specifically, Deng et al. (2017) prepared heteroatom-doped lamellar-structured carbon material derived from alfalfa leaf, which showed excellent electrochemical activity toward oxygen reduction reaction. Hou (Hou et al. (2019) synthesized a hierarchically porous graphitic material through the hydrothermal treatment and chemical activation of black locust seed dregs. An excellent specific capacitance and a high capability in alkaline electrolyte were achieved. Li et al. (2019) combined the slow pyrolysis of a dry bamboo waste with either two-step or one-step chemical activation and modification to produce activated carbons with a specific surface area and nitrogen contents desirable for achieving high performance in electrocatalysis in CO2 reduction. The modification method and activate reagents positively affected its physicochemical structure, porosity, N-doping concentrations, doped N types, and the electrochemical behavior of the resulting metal-free carbon catalysts. Although some progress has been made for the carbon catalysts derived from different biomasses for various energy-related and environmental applications, little work has been conducted on fabricating biomass-derived metal-fee carbocatalysts toward the electrochemical reduction of CO2.

Municipal sludge is a type of unwanted waste biomass from wastewater treatment with a huge amount, which is considered to possess high electrocatalytic activity due to its relatively high contents of N and transition metal. Then, municipal sludge was exploited to fabricate an effective and efficient catalyst for the electrochemical conversion of CO2 in this study. By one-step high-temperature pyrolysis or a two-step process involving low-temperature carbonization and high-temperature pyrolysis, municipal sludge can be directly converted to a carbon-based material which consisting of two kinds of carbon materials: N-doped carbon and carbon dots (CDs). The CDs were anchored across the N-doped carbon surface, forming a composite structure. The microstructure and properties of the as-prepared CDs-decorated, N-doped hierarchical biocarbon catalysts were unveiled, and their electrocatalytic activities for CO2 reduction were systematically studied. The origin of the carbocatalysts catalytic activity for CO2 reduction was clarified by further employing of density functional theory (DFT). This study opens up an effective method to the integration of various carbon-based electrocatalysts derived from waste biomasses for the electrochemical reduction of CO2 to high value-added organic products.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs-decorated, N-doped biocarbon materials
ce:section.ce:para: Municipal sludge employed in this study was obtained from Guangzhou Liede wastewater treatment plant. At first, the as-obtained municipal sludge was drained through a filter paper and dried at 60 °C for 24 h, and the ultimate analysis and proximate analysis of the municipal sludge sample are shown in Table S1. Next, the dried municipal sludge was pyrolyzed at 900 °C for 2 h under N2 flowing conditions, which was followed by naturally cooling at ambient temperature to produce biocarbon (named as MSC hereafter). Afterward, MSC was steeped and stirred magnetically in a 6 mol/L HCl solution for 24 h to remove trace metals. Then, the acid-treated sample was further rinsed by with deionized water until the filtrate was neutral, The washed sample was filtered and finally dried at 80 °C to constant weight, resulting in the sample labeled as MSC-A.

For comparison, dried municipal sludge (about 5 g) and deionized water (about 40 mL) were loaded into a 50 mL Teflon-lined vessel (QiangQiang Instrument, Shanghai), heated to 180 °C, and held for 24 h. Following heating, the black solid product was obtained and collected by centrifugal separation and oven-dried at 60 °C. The residual solution was also gathered for further examination to disclose the composition. Then, the dried black solid product was pyrolyzed following the same operating procedures mentioned above, and the resultant biocarbon materials before and after further acid treatment were labeled as MSC-H and MSC-HA, respectively.

The morphology and structure of the CDs-decorated, N-doped hierarchical biocarbon was investigated by field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) and high-resolution transmission electronmicroscope (HRTEM). Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms were measured using a sorptometer operated at 77 K, and then the specific surface area was estimated by the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller method, while Barrett– Joyner–Halenda (BJH) method was used to analyze the distribution of pores. The XRD patterns of the municipal sludge-derived biocarbon materials were obtained using an X-ray diffractometer. Raman spectra were recorded with a LabRAM HR800 Aramis confocal microscope Raman spectrometer system. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) investigation was conducted with an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer, and the binding energy values for XPS was calibrated by C1s 284.6 eV.

In situ Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was recorded using a Nexus 6700 spectrometer equipped with a liquid nitrogen-cooled MCT-A detector. A prismatic CaF2 disk window worked as a spectroelectrochemical cell, while gold disks coated with the as-prepared biocarbon were employed as the working electrode. A thin electrolyte layer was formed because the working electrode was pushed against the window in the spectroelectrochemical cell during the in situ FTIR spectrum measurement. The FTIR spectra were recorded with about 400 scans and potential control at a resolution of about 8 cm−1. A multi-stepped FTIR spectroscopic technique was also implemented to track the variation of the characteristic functional groups with the CO2 reduction duration.

All of the electrochemical surveys were performed in a three-electrode setup at ambient pressure and temperature. A glassy carbon electrode coated with the as-prepared biocarbon material was employed as the working electrode, prepared based on the following procedures. The as-prepared biocarbon material (20 mg) was mixed with Nafion solution (5 μL) and ethanol (1 mL) by ultrasonication for about 30 min to make a homogeneous mixture which was then coated on the glassy carbon electrode before natural drying treatment. The Pt and Ag/AgCl worked as counter and reference electrodes, respectively. For comparison, the corresponding value was transformed based on the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE). Cyclic Voltammetry (CV) and Linear Sweep Voltammetry (LSV) of the as-prepared biocarbon materials was conducted in an Ar or CO2-saturated KHCO3 solution (0.1 mol/L, pH 6.8) at a scan rate of 10 mVs−1. Before each test, a 0.1 mol/L KHCO3 electrolyte was bubbled with CO2 for 30 min in advance.

The electrochemical reduction of CO2 was conducted in a KHCO3 solution (0.1 mol/L, pH 6.8) with a gas-tight one-compartment electrochemical cell (100 mL) at ambient pressure and temperature (Figure S1). A Pt plate, Ag/AgCl, and a graphite plate incorporating the as-prepared biocarbon (the loading density of 5 mg/cm2) were adopted as the counter, reference, and working electrodes, respectively. The electrolysis was conducted potentiostatically over the potential range from -1.1 to -1.5 V, and the electrochemical cell was continuously flushed with the CO2 gas.

For each applied voltage, the electrolysis was performed for 10 h. The effluent gas stream was collected by an air pocket. The gaseous products were periodically measured using a Refinery gas chromatograph equipped with an FID and two TCD detectors. Five columns were used for gas separation, and a standard method was employed for quantitative analysis of the gas product. For the measurement of the liquid product, 1 mL electrolyte was collected at each applied voltage, progressively blended with 0.2 mL D2O and 0.05 mL acetone, and then analyzed and quantified with a 1H nuclear magnetic resonance spectrometer (VANCE III400).

DFT calculations were carried out by the Dmol3 code (Delley et al., 2000). The double numerical quality basis set and the polarization with p-functions on hydrogen atoms were used based on the GGA-PBE method (Perdew et al., 1996a; Perdew et al., 1996b). The 4 × 4 × 1 K-point mesh was set by the Monkhorst-Pack method and a 3.7 Å orbital cutoff was performed. A 0.005 Ha smearing was applied to promote the self-consistent field convergence. The convergence criteria of force, energy and displacement were set to less than 0.002 Ha/atom, 10−5 Ha and 0.005 Å, respectively.

The reaction energy for each electrochemical step of a proton-electron transfer was calculated based on the computational hydrogen electrode (CHE) model (Nørskov et al., 2005), where zero voltage (vs. RHE) was applied. The variation in Gibbs free energy (ΔG) for each procedure could be calculated by Eq. (1-(3):

where ∆E represents the energy difference of adsorption, ∆S refers to the entropy change, ∆EZPE designates zero-point energy, U (U=0) represents the potential at the standard hydrogen electrode, e stands for the charge transfer, and ∆GpH represents the Gibbs free energy normalized by the H+ concentration (pH 0). While DFT calculations yielded ∆E, the ∆EZPE and ∆S were calculated based on the standard thermodynamic data (T=298.15 K).

The detailed procedures to synthesize CDs decorated, N-doped biocarbon materials (MSC-HA) are provided in the experiment and methods part. The subsequent acid treatment in a 6 M HCl solution for 24 h allowed the trace metals in biocarbon materials to be almost entirely removed (Table S2). The Al and Si residues were reported to promote hydrogen evolution reduction (HER) at -2.2 V vs. SCE in a 0.05 M KHCO3 electrolyte, with typical current efficiencies of 95.7% and 102.2%, indicating a negative performance toward catalyzing carbon dioxide reduction (Azuma et al., 1993). The morphologies of the as-prepared biocarbon materials were first observed by SEM. According to Fig. 1 and Figure S2, MSC, MSC-A, MSC-H, and MSC-HA exhibit primary amorphous particles and granules. The comparison between the samples before and after acid treatment, reveals that the acid treatment has a limited influence on its morphology. It is worth mentioning that spherical nanoparticles can be observed on the biocarbon surface in a high-resolution SEM image (inset of Fig. 1b), and their quantity is notably increased after the hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C (inset of Fig. 1d). The feature of the spherical nanoparticles was further examined by TEM and HRTEM (Fig. 1e). TEM images confirm the presence of spherical nanoparticles anchored on the biocarbon material surface. The HRTEM image presented in the inset of Fig. 1e verifies that these spherical nanoparticles are considered to be CDs with a size of 2–7 nm and a lattice spacing of about 0.33 nm, which may be considered as few-layer carbon nanoparticle or graphitic crystallites (Hu et al., 2015). The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of MSC-HA shows a ring-like profile with many diffused bright blobs (Fig. 1f), which can be partially ascribed to the presence of CDs with plentiful edges and nitrogen doping defects (Hu et al., 2015). In addition, the inset of Fig. 1f demonstrates that the remaining solution collected after the hydrothermal disposal exhibits bright blue luminescence, which consolidates the existence of CDs. For the CD generation, previous studies demonstrated that fluorescent CDs could be derived from the conversion of bacterial cells (Zhou et al., 2016a) and glucose (Yang et al., 2011). Thus, we speculate that the CDs in the as-prepared biocarbon materials are probably originated from the microorganisms, as a rich composition in the municipal sludge. Since the hydrothermal treatment has been demonstrated to produce a large number of carbon nanoparticles (Zhou et al., 2016b), an increase in the number of CDs can also be achieved via regulating the hydrothermal treatment in our case.

The XRD patterns of all the as-prepared biocarbon materials display two main characteristic diffraction peaks at 19.5° and 25.3°, which can be assigned to the (100) facet of silicon dioxide and (002) facet of hexagonal graphite, respectively (Figure S3). For the MSC and MSC-H samples, the XRD peaks can be noted at approximately 35.3°, 40.4°, 44.3°, 47.4°, and 54°, indicating the presence of the Fe2P phase (PDF# 01–087–2337). The peaks at 38.8°, 40.8 °, 47.2°, and 73.4° can be due to the presence of aluminum compounds (PDF# 01–087–2337). However, for MSC-A and MSC-HA, no peaks mentioned above appear, revealing that the acid treatment removes the trace metallic species. It is also worth noting that the acid treatment enables the specific surface areas and pore diameter of the biocarbon materials to be increased. As shown in Figure S4a and Table 1 , N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of MSC, MSC-H, MSC-A, and MSC-HA show an inconspicuous hysteresis loop. Such N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms can be assigned to the IV/V type based on the IUPAC classification, indicating the presence of many slot holes shaped by the irregular accumulation of the loose polymer lamellar particles. The pore diameter distribution plots of the CDs decorated, N-doped biocarbons materials were obtained by the Barrett–Joyner–Halenda approach, and all of them exhibit peaks at 1 nm and 26 nm (Figure S4b). Notably, the acid treatment enriches the micropores, and the specific surface area is also increased from 75.93 (MSC) and 93.63 (MSC-H) to 103.22 (MSC-A) and 141.17 (MSC-HA) m2/g, respectively.

Fig. 1 g shows the Raman spectra, MSC, MSC-H, MSC-A, and MSC-HA, all of which exhibit two prominent Raman bands around ∼1336 cm−1 and ∼1585 cm−1, which are considered to be the characteristic D and G bands of carbon, respectively (Li et al., 2016). The D peak is identified as the disordered sp2 hybridized carbons, indicative of the disordered graphite structure. In contrast, the G peak is correlated with the in-plane bond stretching motion of the pairs of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms, indicating the existence of the crystalline graphitic carbon. The disarrangement extent in materials can be reflected by D-to-G band intensity ratio (ID/IG) (Gao et al., 2014). The ID/IG can be estimated to be about 2.92, 3.21, 3.11 and 3.24 for the MSC, MSC-H, MSC-A, and MSC-HA samples, respectively. Compared with other samples, the MSC-HA is more defective, as evidenced by the larger ID/IG, which may arise from the combined hydrothermal and acid treatment during its preparation.

XPS was employed to check the chemical structures of MSC, MSC-H, MSC-A and MSC-HA. The full-range XPS analysis (shown in Figures S5a, S6a, S7a, and S8a) confirms the existence of C, O, and N in MSC, MSC-H, MSC-A, and MSC-HA, and a trace amount of Fe and P can be detected in MSC and MSC-H, which is consistence with the results shown in Table S2. The N content of MSC-HA (∼4.79%) is significantly higher than that of MSC (∼3.05%), MSC-H (∼4.58%) and MSC-A (∼3.53%), which may be attributed to the hydrothermal treatment that results in more CDs and preserves more N atoms. The specified structural natures of these elements can be detected by high-resolution XPS. In Figures S5b, S6b, S7b, and S8b, the high-resolution C 1 s spectra of MSC, MSC-H, MSC-A and MSC-HA are provide over the range of 280 to 290 eV. The binding energies of 284.1, 284.6, and 285.3 eV can be assigned to C-H and C-C, respectively (Ago et al., 1999). As reported by Gong et al., carbon atoms on the edge site can induce electron transfer, endowing them with more negative charges. Consequently, the adsorption of O2 molecules occurs more easily on edge carbon relative to basal carbon (Gong et al., 2009; Zhong et al., 2014). Like oxygen reduction reactions, carbon in the as-prepared materials may result in a similar tendency toward the electrochemical conversion of CO2. In the high-resolution O 1 s spectrum of MSC, MSC-H, MSC-A, and MSC-HA (Figures S5c, S6c, S7c, and S8c), three peaks can be observed: 530.9–531.8 eV (O=C), 531.9–532.8 eV (O-H), and 532.9–533.3 eV (O-C) (Tan et al., 2012; Serov et al., 2014). According to Zhang et al., O-doping may facilitate H2 production, while N-doping plays a nontrivial role in CO2 reduction (Zhang et al., 2014). However, the as-prepared biocarbon is comprised of a highly complex matrix with various components, so the exact role of oxygen needs to be further studied in future work by considering the limited length of one article. As depicted in Fig. 1h, four types of N functionalities can be deconvoluted at 398.0 ± 0.3 eV, 400.4 ± 0.3 eV, 401.1 ± 0.3 eV, and 402.0 ± 0.3 eV, which can be assigned to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, graphitic N, and oxidized pyridinic N (N-oxide), respectively (Serov et al., 2014; Su et al., 2014). According to Zhu et al. (2016), nitrogen heteroatoms with high electronegativity likely cause the neighboring carbon atoms to be positively charged. Thus, such carbon atoms are generally regarded as the active site for electrochemical conversion of CO2. Besides, previous studies demonstrated that the pyridinic N combined with two carbon atoms at the margin of a carbon plane and the carbon vacancy, resulted in active sites for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and facilitated molecular O2 adsorption at the same time (Jia et al., 2018). Similar to ORR, pyridinic/pyrrolic N may result in a high electron density, which is essential for CO* dimerization, and the acidic CO2 molecule tends to adsorb onto the pyridinic N-containing functional group within the carbon nanostructures. As a result, pyridinic/pyrrolic N may also serve as active sites for the electrochemical conversion of CO2 (Song et al., 2017; Wu et al., 2015). The total N and pyridinic N contents in MSC are estimated to be 3.05% and 0.53% (Fig. 1i and Table S2), which increase to 3.53% and 0.69% after acid treatment, respectively. After hydrothermal treatment, the total N and pyridinic N contents in MSC-H are further elevated to 4.58% and 1.16% receptively, and MSC-HA possesses the highest total N and pyridinic N contents of ∼4.79% and ∼1.27%, receptively. It is confirmed that hydrothermal and acid treatments are favorable for reserving and exposing more N groups. Although Fe 2p and P 2p are detectable in MSC and MSC-H (Figures S5, S6, S7 and S8), they cannot be probed in MSC-A and MSC-HA, implying that the acid treatment is a viable approach for the purification of CDs-decorated, N-doped hierarchical biocarbon materials. In the high-resolution XPS P 2p spectra, the characteristic peaks can be found at 133.6 ± 0.2 eV and 134.8 ± 0.2 eV, which may be considered P-C and P-O, respectively. And according to Strelko et al., (2000), P doping of carbon materials improves the electron donor properties and promotes catalytic activity. Two primary peaks at 712.2 ± 0.2 and 725.3 ± 0.2 eV can be indexed to Fe 2p. These binding energies can be separated into four peaks at 711.0 ± 0.3 eV, 715.1 ± 0.3 eV, 724.1 ± 0.3 eV and 729.1 ±0 0.3 eV, which can be attributed to Fe3+ in FeOM (M repsents metal elements) and FeOH, as well as the Fe2+ and Fe3+ in the acicular structure (Yuan et al., 2016). Moreover, sewage sludge-derived biochar was demonstrated to be rich in inorganic materials, and such a character makes it become a possible candidate as metal-free catalyst (Norouzi et al., 2019), thus indicating that the as-prepared metal-free MSC-H might also possess a superior activity in the electrochemical reduction of CO2.

To discover the electrochemical properties of the CDs decorated, N-doped hierarchical biocarbon materials, we examined the CV and LSV curves at ambient atmosphere in an Ar-saturated or CO2-saturated 0.1 mol/L KHCO3 electrolyte. MSC-H exhibits almost no cathodic currents in the Ar-saturated KHCO3 aqueous solution until -0.88 V (vs. RHE) (Figure S9). The onset overpotential for the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) is much higher than the apparent standard cell potential (-0.41 V vs. RHE) for the HER, indicating the HER occurs with the need for a much more negative potential in the case of the prepared biocarbon materials (Wu et al., 2015). In the CO2-saturated KHCO3 electrolyte, the reduction current is significantly increased due to CO2 reduction and HER. It is worth mentioning that, an apparent current density of 16.55 mA/cm2 can be obtained with MSC-HA, which is 15 times higher than that of the silver nanoparticle electrocatalyst (Salehi-Khojin et al., 2013) and comparable to that of the nitrogen-doped carbon-coated multiwall carbon nanotube (MWCNT) nanocomposites (Jhong et al., 2016).

To further detect the electrocatalytic property of the as-prepared hierarchical biocarbon materials, we measured the LSV curves with the scan rate of 10 mV/s in the Ar-saturated or CO2-saturated 0.1 mol/L KHCO3 electrolyte (Fig. 2 ). The noticeable difference in the LSV curves for the as-prepared biocarbon materials in the CO2-saturated electrolyte and that in the Ar-saturated electrolyte reveals the electrochemical reduction of CO2. The onset potentials for MSC-H and MSC-HA are measured to be -0.38 and -0.40 V, much more positive than those of MSC (-0.57 V) and MSC-A (-0.59 V) (Table 1). For the electrochemical conversion of CO2 with MSC-H, the net current density keeps increased to the highest value (24.23 mA/cm2) at the potential of -1.01 V, with an onset potential observed at -0.57 V and a current plateau at -0.88 V. The net current density value (obtained from MSC-H) is followed by MSC-HA (18.52 mA/cm2), MSC (8.90 mA/cm2), and MSC-A (4.18 mA/cm2). The best performance in the electrochemical conversion of CO2 exhibited by MSC-H in terms of both net current density and onset potential can be ascribed to more active sites that are accessible for the catalytic reaction, which is strongly associated with the improved N content (especially pyridinic N), specific surface area, and quantities of CDs. These results further demonstrate that the acid treatment is sufficient to remove trace metallic species in MSC-A and MSC-HA. Despite that the biocarbon materials exhibit a slightly lower catalytic performance than the samples containing metallic species, they are still capable of catalyzing the CO2 reduction at the same level due to an abundance of catalytic sites generated by N doping and CD decoration.

Since CO2 reduction is often accompanied by HER (e.g., the solubilization of CO2 in aquesous electrolytes lowers the pH and hence enhances the HER), a change in the reduction current is not the explicit evidence for CO2 reduction. Consequently, the reduction products were detected to confirm whether they were generated by CO2 reduction. The electrolysis of CO2 in the presence of MSC, MSC-A, MSC-H, and MSC-HA was carried out at the external potential of -0.71 V vs. RHE, and the H2 production rate appeared to be about 8.46 μg h−1, 7.53 μg h−1. 9.02 μg h−1, and 7.10 μg h−1, with the corresponding faraday efficiencies (FEs) calculated to about 4.33%, 3.87%, 5.23%, and 3.54%, respectively. Moreover, except H2 generated from the parasite HER, C2H5OH, CH3OH, and CH4 are detectable in the CO2 reduction products. For C2H5OH products, the production rates can be calculated to be about 72.33 μg h−1 cm−2, 37.56 μg h−1 cm−2, 117.01 μg h−1 cm−2, and 111.17 μg h−1 cm−2 for MSC, MSC-A, MSC-H, and MSC-HA, respectively, which are 3.18, 3.07, 3.01 and 3.12 times higher than that for CH3OH, and 5.38, 1.63, 5.35 and 6.63 times higher than that for CH4 (Fig. 3 a). The result confirms that the electrochemical conversion of CO2 leads to the production of alcohol products with relatively higher selectivity to ethanol. Such selectivity may arise from the adequate N configurations, especially the pyridinic N. According to Li et al., N doping at the basal planes required much higher formation energy than that at the edge sites, implying that N (especially pyridinic N) is preferred to locate at the edge sites of carbon-based materials (Li et al., 2009). Furthermore, pyridinic N at the basal plane of the carbon material is more inert than that at the edge site (J. Wu et al., 2016; Shen et al., 2014), which is thereby presumed as the main active sites that induce the C-C bond formation and result in a higher yield of C2 and C3 organic products. In Fig. 3b, the corresponding FEs reveal the same tendency as that of electrocatalytic activities, and MSC-H exhibits the highest value of 93.29%, relative to that of MSC (62.89%), MSC-A (55.14%), and MSC-HA (90.14%). The possible reasons for such a tendency is described as follows: pyridinic-N and CDs in the as-prepared biocarbon materials work as the active sites to catalyze CO2 reduction. After acid treatment, the catalytic activity is degraded slightly due to a loss of the trace metals of Fe and Al (J. Wu et al., 2016; Wu et al., 2015; Fernandes et al., 2019).

Production rates and corresponding FEs of C2H5OH, CH3OH and CH4 in MSC-HA at different given potentials are presented in Fig. 3c and d. With the external potentials increases from -1.1 V to -1.3 V, the production rates and corresponding FEs of C2H5OH and CH3OH increase accordingly, in contrast to that of CH4, which remains unchanged or decreased slightly. These results indicate that an increased external potential may promote the production of C2H5OH and CH3OH, but otherwise inhibit the production of CH4 (Liu et al., 2017; Wu et al., 2015). Subsequently, when the external potentials are further improved to -1.4 and -1.5 V, both the CO2 electrochemical reduction and HER are enhanced, and HER is more kinetically favorable under this condition. Then, HER recovers to be dominant, meanwhile, the electrochemical reduction of CO2 becomes slower (Li et al., 2017; Ma et al., 2016b), leading to the contrary results that the production rates of C2H5OH, CH3OH, and CH4 are gradually decreased. Overall, the as-prepared biocarbon material (i.e., MSC-HA) presents the best catalytic performance at the potential of -0.71 V, with maximum production rates for C2H5OH, CH3OH, and CH4 measured to be 111.17 μg h−1 cm−2, 35.66 μg h−1 cm−2, and 16.77 μg h−1 cm−2, respectively, and total FEs calculated up to 90.14%, much higher than that under -0.51 V (64.22%), -0.61 V (70.11%), -0.81 V (74.98%) and -0.91 V (67.39%). Compared with the artificial electrocatalytic materials, the as-prepared MSC-HA with larger Raman ID/IG, CDs decoration and in situ N-doping are highly promising as a candidate for the fabrication of high- performance electrode for CO2RR. Three main value-added organic products (C2H5OH, CH3OH, and CH4) were obtained with a total FEs of 90.14%, which is comparable to or even higher than many previously reported electrocatalysts (Table S3). The time-dependent current density curves for MSC-HA at the corresponding potential displayed in Figure S10 indicate that the electrochemical reduction of CO2 occurs at the potential of -0.51 to -0.91 V along with stable current densities.

Stability is a crucial indicator in judging the catalytic performance of the as-prepared biocarbon material. The stability of MSC-HA was detected by six consecutive cycling runs with each run lasting for 5 h in the CO2-saturated KHCO3 aqueous solution at -0.71 V vs. RHE. As shown in Fig. 4 a, both production rates and FEs of MSC-HA have no apparent changes during the six consecutive cycling runs, indicating the high stability of MSC-HA towards the electrochemical reduction of CO2. Fig. 4b provides the plot of C2H5OH, CH3OH, and CH4 production yields vs. reaction time at -0.71 V on MSC-HA. The quantity of the detected products is linearly increased with the electrocatalytic reaction time. A 30 h electrochemical stability test on MSC-HA at -0.71 V shows a very mild change in the current density (Fig. 4d). All of the test results reveal that MSC-HA exhibits excellent durability for the electrochemical conversion of CO2. Furthermore, CO2 reduction performance of MSC-HA at -0.71 V with 5 h cycles alternatingly varied between the CO2-saturated and Ar-saturated electrolytes over a total duration of 30 h was further evaluated (Fig. 4c), and C2H5OH, CH3OH, and CH4 could be detected in the CO2-saturated electrolytes while nothing can be detected in the Ar-saturated electrolytes. The above data imply that the organic products are yielded from CO2 electroreduction rather than the release and decomposition of the as-prepared carbon catalyst. All of the results demonstrate that the municipal sludge-derived MSC-HA is a competent candidate in catalyzing the CO2 reduction to yield organic products of CH3OH, C2H5OH, and CH4, along with excellent stability for all the catalytic reaction cycles.

In order to unravel the possible reasons for the remarkable electrocatalytic activity of the carbon dots-decorated, N-doped hierarchical biocarbon material and to clarify the activity of different N configurations for the electrochemical conversion of CO2 to C2H5OH, DFT calculations were conducted on the (002) graphite surface. According to Kortlever et al, the rate-determining steps (RDSs) for CO2 reduction in a C2 pathway are described as follows: (i) the generation of the adsorbed intermediates, that is, *COOH (RDS1), and (ii) the proton-coupled electron-transfer reduction of *CO to *COCO (RDS2) (Song et al., 2017; Kortlever et al., 2015). Pure carbon is considered inactive for the electroreduction of CO2 due to a lack of sufficient electrons for the adsorption of CO2 and the formation of *CO intermediate, whereas N -doping can promote the adsorption/activation of CO2 and consequently overcome the energy barrier of RDSs (Song et al., 2017).

Figs. 5 , S11, S12, and S13 show the free energy diagram of CO2 conversion to C2H5OH over the pyrrolic N, pyridinic N and graphitic N sites. It can be noted that the reaction energies for the *COOH formation on the pyrrolic N, pyridinic N and graphitic N sites are calculated as -0.01, 0.69 and 2.31 eV, respectively. The highest free energy barrier (2.31 eV) of the RDS1 indicates the hindered electrochemical conversion of CO2 on the graphitic N sites. By contrast, the catalytic reaction is favorable on the pyrrolic N and pyridinic N sites, with lower free energy barriers of -0.01 and 0.69 eV, in agreement with previous results (Song et al., 2017; Wu et al., 2016b)..Moreover, the reaction energies required for the *COCO formation on the pyridinic N sites are calculated to be ∼2.00 eV, comparable to pyrrolic N (∼2.13 eV) and much lower than that of graphitic N sites (∼3.50 eV). The corresponding free energy barriers of RDS2 over the pyrrolic N, pyridinic N and graphitic N sites are estimated to be about 0.42, -1.47 and 2.47 eV, respectively, indicating that the pyridinic N is the most competent N configuration for RDS2, followed by the pyrrolic N and graphitic N. Notably, the negative free energy barrier on the pyridinic N site reveals that the *CO dimerization step can occur spontaneously. The C-C coupling reaction can thereby quickly occur over the MSC-HA catalyst with the help of pyridinic N. The subsequent *COCO reduction process has been reported to be mainly dictated by the addition of H+, and the elementary reaction involving hydrogenation can be promoted as H+ species prefer to adsorb on the doped N configurations. Both the experimental study and DFT calculation prove results displayed that the incorporation of highly active N sites (especially the pyridinic N and pyrrolic N sites) has a crucial contribution to the effective and selective electrocatalysis of CO2 reduction for ethanol production. These results are mainly due to the electron-rich surface imparted via the N doping, which can supply sufficient electrons to adsorb CO2 and stabilize the *CO intermediates (Song et al., 2017; Wu et al., 2015; Wu et al., 2016b). The energetically possible configurations combined with the elementary procedures of the electrochemical conversion of CO2 on the pyridinic N site are shown in Fig. 5b.

Favorable pathways for multi-electron CO2 electrochemical reduction on MSC-HA-coated electrode were monitored by an in situ FTIR spectroscopy. A time-dependent FTIR spectrum for CO2 reduction on an MSC-HA-coated electrode at -0.71 V vs. RHE is presented in Fig. 6 , with the CO2-saturated KHCO3 aqueous electrolyte as the background. No peaks can be found before the electrochemical reduction reaction. The peak that appears at approximately 1030 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of the C-OH group (Collins et al., 2005), and its intensity increases with the electrolysis time, demonstrating the accumulation of alcohol products. The band at about 1232∼1342 cm−1 is attributed to –CH2 (Solomons et al., 2001). The peaks related to COO- and C=O can be verified at about 1527 cm−1 and 1753 cm−1, respectively (Solomons et al., 2001; Liu et al., 2015), and their intensities change irregularly, suggesting that COO- and C=O may be the intermediate generated during the electrochemical conversion of CO2. The peak intensity (around 2339 cm−1 and 2366 cm−1) increases with the electrolysis time, suggesting the gradual adsorption of CO2 onto the MSC-HA-coated electrode. Based on the results obtained via the in situ FTIR spectrum and the detection of the product, along with the previously reported mechanism (Kortlever et al., 2015), we present a favorable mechanism for the CO2 reduction on the MSC-HA-coated electrode, as depicted in Fig. 6b. At first, CO2 molecules are primarily adsorbed onto the carbon surface to form *CO2, which is then reduced to *CO2 − radicals or generates the adsorbed intermediate *COOH. Afterward, *COOH is reduced to *CO in the presence of H+ and e−. Consequently, the *CO is protonated and reduced to CH4 or CH3OH. On the other hand, the integration of two neighboring *CO species into *COCO, coupled with protonation reactions, *COCO is finally reduced to C2H5OH.

In summary, a CDs-decorated, N-doped hierarchical biocarbon material has been developed starting from municipal sludge for the highly effective and alternative electrochemical conversion of CO2 to value-added organic products. C2H5OH, CH3OH, and CH4 are obtained with the total faraday efficiency of 90.14% at -0.71 V vs. RHE, and the as-prepared biocarbon material exhibits an excellent stability during the whole electrocatalytic reaction cycles. The experimental analysis combined with DFT calculation demonstrates the decoration of CDs and the incorporation of active N sites, especially the pyridinic N and pyrrolic N sites, play a pivotal role in the effective and alternative production of C2H5OH. The potential reaction pathway is disclosed for the electrochemical conversion of CO2 as follows: CO2 → *CO2 → *COOH → *CO → CH4/CH3OH or CO2 → *CO2 → *COOH → *CO → *COCO → *COCOH → *COCHOH → *COCH2OH → *CHOCH2OH → *CH2OCH2OH → CH3CH2OH. The *COCO species can be easily formed with the help of pyridinic-N. Our findings open up an avenue to explore various biocarbon electrocatalysts derived from diverse biomasses for the electrochemical conversion of CO2 with excellent efficiency and selectivity.

Lifang Deng conceived the idea, performed the experiments, analyzed the results and drafted the manuscript. Xin Qian and Lufeng Wang assisted in completing part of the experiment. Qiang Lu and Huawen Hu offered advice and writing guidance for this job. Haoran Yuan and Yong Chen supervised the project, designed the experiments, and co-drafted the manuscript. All authors contributed to the analysis and revision of the paper.
